Primary customers: Participating breweries/bottlers (Lion, DB) as scheme members and beneficiaries; New Zealand beer consumers as end users returning bottles; retail and hospitality venues as return points.
Ownership status: Owned by scheme members Lion and DB (major NZ brewers); logistics run by Associated Bottlers Co.; promoted/coordinated via the Glass Packaging Forum (industry-funded, accredited product stewardship non-profit, established 2006)
Core model: The scheme standardises beer bottle design across participating brewers, enabling any returned bottle to be washed and refilled by any participating bottler rather than requiring brand-specific bottle matching. Consumers return empty bottles (commonly in branded "Swappa" crates) via retail and hospitality return points; bottles are collected through existing beverage-distribution reverse logistics, inspected, washed, and refilled at bottling facilities around the country, with unsuitable bottles sent to recycling. Short summary: The "Swappa Crate" is New Zealand's original and longest-running refill scheme, built around standardised, reusable glass beer bottles that originated with the Auckland Bottling Company (ABC) in 1920. Geographic scope: United Kingdom (London; registered office Runcorn, England)

Primary customers: Mass-market consumers, sold via SodaStream's own retail/e-commerce channels and third-party retailers (supermarkets, department stores, e-commerce) in 40+ countries.

Core model: SodaStream sells a B2C razor-and-blades model: consumers buy a home carbonation machine and reusable CO2 cylinders, then return empty cylinders for exchange (via SodaStream's own retail/online exchange program or third-party retail exchange points) rather than buying new ones — each returned cylinder is inspected, sterilized and refilled through what the company calls its "Triple Quality Control" process before re-entering circulation. This cylinder-exchange loop is the company's core reuse mechanism and displaces both single-use beverage packaging (bottles/cans) and single-use gas canisters.
